# UC-10.12.58 · VPN Split-Tunnel Risk Scoring with Application Correlation

## Description

Correlates remote-access tunnel start events that advertise split-tunnel posture with proxied direct-internet browsing volume per user, surfacing identities where sensitive corporate traffic may share a host with under-monitored breakout paths.

## Value

Security architects can quantify which remote sessions undermine full-tunnel protections, informing policy waivers, agent upgrades, conditional access prompts, or targeted awareness before regulated data egress paths multiply unseen.

## Implementation

Normalize `user` identity between VPN logs and proxy realms (often UPN versus short name lookups). Maintain `split_tunnel` field extraction parity across Cisco ASA syslog and Palo Alto GlobalProtect versions. Tune direct_web_request ceilings using enterprise browsing baselines sampled over thirty days.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Splunk Palo Alto Networks Add-on and Cisco ASA Add-on forwarding identity-rich GlobalProtect or AnyConnect transcripts into `vpn` indexes.
• Broadcom ProxySG or Symantec-derived `bluecoat:proxysg` events with correlated `cs_host` domains and user identities.
• Identity lookup aligning VPN `user`, `samaccountname`, and proxy `user` fields (CSV or KV store).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Retain both tunnel-establishment and teardown lines; prefer JSON exports when available so `split_tunnel` is explicit. Normalize timestamps to correlate ± two minutes skew between VPN and proxy clusters. Exclude known pilot cohorts flagged in a KV store lookup during early tuning.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Iterate risk_score boundaries with insider-risk stakeholders:

```spl
index=vpn sourcetype IN ("pan:globalprotect","cisco:asa") action="tunnel-start"
| eval source_ip_str=coalesce(public_ip, client_public_ip, src_ip,"unknown")
| join type=left user [
    search index=proxy sourcetype=bluecoat:proxysg
    | stats count as direct_web_requests dc(cs_host) as unique_sites by user ]
| fillnull direct_web_requests value=0
| eval risk_score=case(
        split_tunnel="yes" AND direct_web_requests>100,"high",
        split_tunnel="yes" AND direct_web_requests>20,"medium",
        1=1,"low"
      )
| eval risk_priority=case(risk_score="high",3,risk_score="medium",2,1,1)
| where risk_score IN ("high","medium")
| stats values(source_ip_str) as source_ips latest(risk_score) as risk latest(direct_web_requests) as web_requests max(risk_priority) as max_pri by user
| sort - max_pri - web_requests
| fields - max_pri
```

Understanding this SPL

**VPN Split-Tunnel Risk Scoring with Application Correlation** tests whether split-tunnel sessions coincide with heavy direct browsing that bypasses corporate inspection paths.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Primary VPN events anchor each row on `user`.
• `join` pulls proxy counters for the same identity; `fillnull` prevents null arithmetic.
• `case` encodes policy bands; `risk_priority` enforces sort order high before medium.
• Final `stats` collapses duplicates from multi-tunnel jitter within the window.

Step 3 — Validate
Pick several high-risk identities and chase sessions in Palo Alto HIP reports plus proxy SIEM dashboards. Confirm `split_tunnel` interpretation matches firewall configuration—not merely client UI advertising.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Pair detections with education for approved split-tunnel roles (creative media, labs). Escalate only when combined with sensitive resource access lookups you maintain separately; avoid punitive automation on browsing alone without policy review.

## SPL

```spl
index=vpn sourcetype IN ("pan:globalprotect","cisco:asa") action="tunnel-start"
| eval source_ip_str=coalesce(public_ip, client_public_ip, src_ip,"unknown")
| join type=left user [
    search index=proxy sourcetype=bluecoat:proxysg
    | stats count as direct_web_requests dc(cs_host) as unique_sites by user ]
| fillnull direct_web_requests value=0
| eval risk_score=case(
        split_tunnel="yes" AND direct_web_requests>100,"high",
        split_tunnel="yes" AND direct_web_requests>20,"medium",
        1=1,"low"
      )
| eval risk_priority=case(risk_score="high",3,risk_score="medium",2,1,1)
| where risk_score IN ("high","medium")
| stats values(source_ip_str) as source_ips latest(risk_score) as risk latest(direct_web_requests) as web_requests max(risk_priority) as max_pri by user
| sort - max_pri - web_requests
| fields - max_pri
```

## Visualization

Table (user, risk, web_requests, summarized source addresses), Pivot heatmap (risk tier versus department), Timeline of correlated tunnel-plus-proxy bursts.

## Known False Positives

Developers routinely generate hundreds of direct requests while tunnels carry only corp assets; videoconferencing clients may prefer split routes; SaaS-heavy roles legitimately offload trusted CDNs leaving enterprise inspection blind spots by design-approved policy.
